title: 🏥 Mean Time to Recovery [v2]
description: DORA Pillar: Mean Time to Recovery — Measures how quickly a team can restore service after a production failure. Elite teams recover in under 1 hour.

list.name: environment
list.label: Environment
list.definition: SELECT DISTINCT environment FROM deployments ORDER BY environment
list.query: SELECT DISTINCT environment FROM deployments ORDER BY environment

panels.description: Data mode banner — shows whether this dashboard displays live GitHub data (green), synthetic seed data (amber), or demo data (blue).

targets.rawSql:
SELECT COALESCE(
  (SELECT
    CASE mode
      WHEN 'live' THEN '📡 Live Data' || COALESCE(' — ' || NULLIF(source_label, ''), '')
      WHEN 'seed' THEN '🌱 Synthetic Seed Data' || COALESCE(' — ' || NULLIF(source_label, ''), '')
      WHEN 'demo' THEN '🎮 Demo Environment' || COALESCE(' — ' || NULLIF(source_label, ''), '')
      ELSE '❓ Unknown Mode'
    END
  FROM data_mode ORDER BY updated_at DESC LIMIT 1),
  '⚙️ Awaiting first sync…'
) AS \"Data Source\"

options.content:
# 🔧 Mean Time to Recovery (MTTR)

**DORA Pillar: Stability**

## What it Measures
Mean Time to Recovery measures how long it takes to restore service after a production failure or incident. It is the second DORA *stability* metric and reflects your team's ability to detect, diagnose, and resolve production issues quickly.

## Why it Matters
Fast recovery reduces the impact of failures on users and the business. Teams with low MTTR can deploy with confidence, knowing that any issues will be resolved quickly. High MTTR often indicates gaps in monitoring, incident response processes, or deployment automation.

## How to Interpret
| Performance Level | Recovery Time | What it Means |
|---|---|---|
| 🟢 Elite | Less than 1 hour | Automated rollbacks, strong monitoring, fast incident response |
| 🟡 High | 1 hour to 1 day | Good processes with some manual intervention |
| 🟠 Medium | 1 day to 1 week | Slow diagnosis or complex recovery procedures |
| 🔴 Low | More than 1 week | Major gaps in monitoring or incident response |

**Incident Resolution Time** tracks issue-based incidents as a supplementary measure.

## How to Take Action
- **If high MTTR**: Implement automated rollback capabilities, improve monitoring and alerting, create runbooks for common failures
- **If incident resolution is slow**: Review incident response processes, ensure on-call coverage, reduce time-to-detection with better alerts
- **If trending up**: Look for increasing system complexity, new failure modes, or reduced team expertise
- **If consistently low**: Your team has strong operational maturity — consider sharing practices as a model for others

panels.description: Mean Time to Recovery — DORA stability pillar. Performance tiers and action guidance.

panels.title: MTTR (median)
panels.description:
### What it measures
Median time from a failed deployment to the next successful deployment in the same environment.

### What insight it offers
Elite: <1h, High: <1d, Medium: <1w, Low: >1w. Faster recovery reduces the blast radius of failures and customer impact.

### ⚠️ Caveats
- Recovery is proxied as the next successful deployment after a failure in the same environment
- Does not use incident issue resolution time

targets.rawSql:
WITH failure_windows AS (
  SELECT
    d.deployment_id,
    d.created_at AS failed_at,
    LEAD(d.created_at) OVER (PARTITION BY d.environment ORDER BY d.created_at) AS recovered_at
  FROM deployments d
  JOIN deployment_statuses ds ON ds.deployment_id = d.deployment_id
  WHERE ds.state = 'failure'
  AND ('$environment' = '__all__' OR d.environment = '$environment')
  AND d.created_at BETWEEN $__timeFrom() AND $__timeTo()
)
SELECT ROUND(PERCENTILE_CONT(0.5) WITHIN GROUP (
  ORDER BY EXTRACT(EPOCH FROM (recovered_at - failed_at)) / 3600.0
)::numeric, 1) AS "MTTR (hours)"
FROM failure_windows
WHERE recovered_at IS NOT NULL

panels.title: P90 MTTR (hours)
panels.description:
### What it measures
90th percentile time to recovery — the recovery time within which 90% of failures are resolved.

### What insight it offers
Reveals worst-case recovery scenarios. A large gap between P50 and P90 MTTR suggests inconsistent incident response practices.

### ⚠️ Caveats
- Based on the same proxy methodology as median MTTR
- Outliers (very long-running failures) can inflate this significantly

targets.rawSql:
WITH failure_windows AS (
  SELECT
    d.deployment_id,
    d.created_at AS failed_at,
    LEAD(d.created_at) OVER (PARTITION BY d.environment ORDER BY d.created_at) AS recovered_at
  FROM deployments d
  JOIN deployment_statuses ds ON ds.deployment_id = d.deployment_id
  WHERE ds.state = 'failure'
  AND ('$environment' = '__all__' OR d.environment = '$environment')
  AND d.created_at BETWEEN $__timeFrom() AND $__timeTo()
)
SELECT ROUND(PERCENTILE_CONT(0.9) WITHIN GROUP (
  ORDER BY EXTRACT(EPOCH FROM (recovered_at - failed_at)) / 3600.0
)::numeric, 1) AS "P90 MTTR (hours)"
FROM failure_windows
WHERE recovered_at IS NOT NULL

panels.title: Incidents Still Open
panels.description:
### What it measures
Count of GitHub Issues labeled 'incident' that are still in open state.

### What insight it offers
Open incidents represent ongoing service degradation. A high count relative to closed incidents suggests slow resolution or inadequate staffing for incident response.

### ⚠️ Caveats
- Includes all open incident issues regardless of creation date
- Requires consistent use of the 'incident' label

targets.rawSql:
SELECT COUNT(*) AS "Incidents Still Open"
FROM issues i
WHERE i.labels::jsonb @> '[{"name":"incident"}]'
  AND i.state = 'open'

panels.title: MTTR over Time
panels.description:
### What it measures
Weekly median MTTR trend for the selected environment.

### What insight it offers
Trending down indicates improving incident response and recovery capabilities over time.

### ⚠️ Caveats
- Weeks with no failures will show no data points
- Recovery is proxied by next successful deployment after failure

targets.rawSql:
WITH failure_windows AS (
  SELECT
    d.created_at AS failed_at,
    LEAD(d.created_at) OVER (PARTITION BY d.environment ORDER BY d.created_at) AS recovered_at
  FROM deployments d
  JOIN deployment_statuses ds ON ds.deployment_id = d.deployment_id
  WHERE ds.state = 'failure'
  AND ('$environment' = '__all__' OR d.environment = '$environment')
  AND d.created_at BETWEEN $__timeFrom() AND $__timeTo()
)
SELECT
  date_trunc('week', failed_at) AS time,
  ROUND(PERCENTILE_CONT(0.5) WITHIN GROUP (
    ORDER BY EXTRACT(EPOCH FROM (recovered_at - failed_at)) / 3600.0
  )::numeric, 1) AS "MTTR P50 (hours)",
  ROUND(PERCENTILE_CONT(0.9) WITHIN GROUP (
    ORDER BY EXTRACT(EPOCH FROM (recovered_at - failed_at)) / 3600.0
  )::numeric, 1) AS "MTTR P90 (hours)"
FROM failure_windows
WHERE recovered_at IS NOT NULL
GROUP BY 1
ORDER BY 1

panels.title: Incident Resolution Time
panels.description:
### What it measures
Weekly median time from incident issue creation to closure (closed_at - created_at in hours).

### What insight it offers
Complement to deployment-based MTTR. Longer resolution times indicate process or tooling gaps in incident management.

### ⚠️ Caveats
- Requires issues to have a closed_at timestamp
- Issue close time may not exactly match service restoration time

targets.rawSql:
SELECT
  date_trunc('week', i.created_at) AS time,
  ROUND(PERCENTILE_CONT(0.5) WITHIN GROUP (
    ORDER BY EXTRACT(EPOCH FROM (i.closed_at - i.created_at)) / 3600.0
  )::numeric, 1) AS "Issue Resolution Time P50 (hours)"
FROM issues i
WHERE i.labels::jsonb @> '[{"name":"incident"}]'
  AND i.closed_at IS NOT NULL
  AND i.created_at BETWEEN $__timeFrom() AND $__timeTo()
GROUP BY 1
ORDER BY 1

panels.title: Recent Failures with Recovery Time
panels.description:
### What it measures
List of recent deployment failures with their estimated recovery time (time until next success in the same environment).

### What insight it offers
Provides an audit trail of failures, helping teams identify patterns in which environments or times of day failures are most costly.

### ⚠️ Caveats
- Capped at 25 rows for performance
- Recovery time is null if no subsequent success was recorded

targets.rawSql:
WITH failure_windows AS (
  SELECT
    d.deployment_id,
    d.environment,
    d.sha,
    d.creator_login,
    d.created_at AS failed_at,
    LEAD(d.created_at) OVER (PARTITION BY d.environment ORDER BY d.created_at) AS recovered_at
  FROM deployments d
  JOIN deployment_statuses ds ON ds.deployment_id = d.deployment_id
  WHERE ds.state = 'failure'
  AND ('$environment' = '__all__' OR d.environment = '$environment')
  AND d.created_at BETWEEN $__timeFrom() AND $__timeTo()
)
SELECT
  sha AS "SHA",
  environment AS "Environment",
  creator_login AS "Creator",
  failed_at AS "Failed At",
  recovered_at AS "Recovered At",
  ROUND(EXTRACT(EPOCH FROM (recovered_at - failed_at)) / 3600.0::numeric, 1) AS "Recovery Time (hours)"
FROM failure_windows
ORDER BY failed_at DESC
LIMIT 25
